dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. What brought you to call 911 tonight?
I woke up with shortness of breath and some chest pressure.
Can you tell me your name, John?
Yes, it's John Smith.
What year is it, John?
What city are we currently in, John?
That would be [CITY].
Just to double-check, can you tell me the name of this street here?
I'm not exactly sure, but I believe it's Maple Street.
We need to assess how you're breathing. Let's start with you taking a deep breath for me.
John is tachypneic, respiratory rate is 30 breaths per minute.
Your exhalations have some coarse sounds in the upper lobes, but inhalations are clear. Good job on those deep breaths.
SpO2 is at 86% on room air.
You mentioned leaning forward while you breathe. Any noticeable chest pressure or discomfort at this moment?
Have you felt this chest tightness especially after using your inhaler?
Yes, I prefer leaning forward a bit for breathing. No, no sweating. Just shortness of breath.
Blood pressure is sitting at 125 over 86.
How many times did you use your inhaler since this morning?
I've used it four times. It gives some relief, but the chest pressure comes back.
On a scale from 0 to 10, where would you rate that chest pressure?
It doesn't feel like pain to me... more like pressure. Maybe a 0.
When did you first notice this shortness of breath?
It started around 1 a.m. I was sleeping and woke up choking.
What medications are you currently taking?
Albuterol, Prinivil, Lasix, spironolactone, and some others.
What's your past medical history, John?
I had a heart attack in 2000. I also have COPD and congestive heart failure.
Those other pills you mentioned-can you tell me what they are?
There's also Atorvastatin.
When did you last eat or drink something earlier?
Probably right before bed, but I'm not 100% certain about the time.
Does the chest pressure move or spread to any part of your body?
No, it's just in the middle and does not go elsewhere.
We're going to take a closer listen of your upper lungs now. Ready for a few more deep breaths?
I'm starting your oxygen at 3 liters via the nasal cannula.
Your SpO2 is now at 96% with oxygen therapy.
Coarse sounds in the upper lungs are still present on exhalation. Your tripoding position is typical for someone with COPD.
Your breathing seems labored, but you're managing it well. Any difficulty with hand or arm movement?
I do feel extra effort going into each breath, but nothing I can't handle.
IV now started in your arm using a 20-gauge needle.
Your pulse is at 115-tachycardic.
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response is noted on the EKG.
Let me try to check your blood sugar now.
Unfortunately, I wasn't able to get a glucose reading.
Do you feel any unusual sensations when you take deep breaths or cough?
It's restrictive when I exhale, but I managed to get up some white mucus earlier.
We're going to run a 4-lead EKG first and then the full 12-lead. Please stay still and calm for a moment.
The 4-lead is done. Let's now do the 12-lead EKG.
The 12-lead is complete and shows signs of an old inferoapical infarct and low QRS voltage.
You're at a GCS of 15, meaning you're alert and fully oriented.
I noticed you just said your medications include Prinivil. What is the full name of that one, just so we document it clearly?
Oh, that's Lisinopril.
Giving you 324 mg of aspirin now.
Based on your COPD, breathing and the EKG changes, we're following the respiratory distress protocol for COPD.
Your SpO2 is at 94% after oxygen and your respiratory rate is getting slightly better at 28.
We're going to take you to [COMPANY_NAME] since it's less crowded due to hospital diversions.
We'll now pivot you onto the stretcher using the roll method.
You're now secured with the stretcher straps. We are ready to go.
We have a 69-year-old male with COPD, chest pressure, and atrial fibrillation with RVR.
Vitals on arrival: 125/86, P 115, SpO2 94% on 3L NC. ASA 324 mg administered; IV established.
SpO2 improved with O2. 12-lead showed signs of old infarct and RVR. Glucose could not be obtained.
Severe shortness of breath, normal complexion, and no diaphoresis are consistent with COPD exacerbation.
Patient was transferred to ED Room 14. No changes or adverse effects occurred during transport.
Patient has a history of COPD, CHF, MI, and Hyperlipidemia and is now with hospital care.
